Question 4:
How did Virupaksha and Mahodara die, and at whose hands?

Answer:
When Ravana set out for the battlefield, all the remaining Rakshasa heroes in Lanka followed him. Among them were Virupaksha, Mahodara, and Mahaparshva. On the battlefield, due to Ravana's heroic exploits, the heads of some Vanaras were severed, the chests of some were shattered, and the limbs of others were cut off. Seeing this situation, Sugriva entrusted the protection of the army camp to Sushena and resolved to fight Ravana. He took a huge tree and advanced. Seeing this, the Rakshasa named Virupaksha descended from his chariot, mounted an elephant, and roaring fearsomely, shot arrows at Sugriva on the battlefield. Sugriva was wounded by those arrows. Recovering immediately, Sugriva roared with anger, took a huge tree, and struck with it. The elephant on which Virupaksha was riding collapsed from that blow. Immediately, Virupaksha took a sword and shield and advanced towards Sugriva. In the ensuing close combat between Sugriva and Virupaksha, Virupaksha, his body drenched in blood, fell to the ground from Sugriva's blow, and blood flowed from his nine orifices like streams of water, and Virupaksha died. Seeing Virupaksha fall, Ravana sent Mahodara against Sugriva. Mahodara also fought with Sugriva for a while like Virupaksha, and finally, his head was severed by Sugriva, and he died.

Question 5:

Answer:

Due to the impact of that night-ranger's arrows, the heads of prominent Vanaras in the Vanara army began to be severed. Seeing his forces thus diminishing, Angada took an iron mace lying nearby and struck Mahaparshva fiercely. From that blow, that Rakshasa, along with his charioteer, lost consciousness and fell from his chariot to the ground. Then Jambavan and Gavaksha took large boulders and destroyed Mahaparshva's chariot-horses and chariot. Then Mahaparshva, regaining consciousness, struck Angada with arrows. Thereafter, Angada took a parigha weapon and hurled it at Mahaparshva. From the impact of that parigha weapon, Mahaparshva's bow and arrows and helmet fell to the ground. Then Mahaparshva hurled a large axe at Angada. That son of Vali dodged that blow and, with his thunderbolt-like fist, shattered Mahaparshva's chest and killed him. Thus Mahaparshva died at the hands of Angada.

Question 4:
When the battle between Rama and Ravana reached its peak, how did Devaraja Indra help Shri Rama?

Answer:

At that time, Shri Rama was fighting from the ground while Ravana fought from a golden chariot. Immediately, the charioteer placed the chariot before Rama, bowed to Shri Rama, and said, 'O Rama! To bring you victory in this battle, Indra has sent this chariot. Moreover, that lord himself has presented you with a divine great bow, armor, arrows, and a sharp Shakti lance. O mighty lord! Ascend this chariot. I will act as your charioteer.' After Matali thus spoke, Shri Rama circumambulated the chariot, bowed to it, and ascended it. Then a wonderful and tumultuous battle ensued between Rama and Ravana. While they were discharging great divine astras at each other, Rama saw a fierce trident weapon discharged by Ravana coming towards him. To counter it, he tried to stop it with a volley of his arrows. But seeing that those arrows were rendered powerless by the intensity of Ravana's trident, Rama immediately discharged the Shakti weapon sent by Indra. Then, by the power of the Shakti weapon, Ravana's trident weapon was rendered ineffective. In this way, Devaraja Indra provided appropriate help to Shri Rama.

Question 5:
When the battle between Rama and Ravana reached its peak, why did Ravana's charioteer turn the chariot aside? What explanation did the charioteer give to Ravana for that?

Answer:
Ravana, having been struck by Shri Rama's blows on the battlefield, was filled with rage, his eyes blazing with fire, and he severely wounded Shri Rama. Then Shri Rama rained a shower of arrows upon Ravana who was near him in front. Desiring to slay his enemy in battle, Shri Rama's skill in discharging astras, his valor and prowess, and his battle-ardor doubled. All astras began to come to the mind of that great being instantly. Noticing this, Rama began to strike Ravana fiercely. Ravana, badly wounded by the stone-throws of the Vanara warriors and by Shri Rama's volley of arrows, his mind becoming distraught, could not draw his bow and counter with astras and shastras. Even though he mustered patience and discharged arrows, as his end was approaching, those arrows were of no use at all. Noticing this, Ravana's charioteer, seeing his dire condition, became cautious and slowly turned the chariot to another side. To Ravana, who was angered by this, the charioteer said, 'O lord! The horses are thirsty. I noticed that you need some rest, so I turned the chariot aside. There is nothing else. Now, according to your command, I will place the chariot before Rama.' And the charioteer did accordingly.

Question 4:
Why did Shri Rama fall into deep thought regarding the destruction of Ravana?

Answer:
Both Rama and Ravana, filled with rage, were provoking each other on the battlefield from their chariots, seeking to slay each other, and their forms were extremely terrifying. The Vanaras and Rakshasas stopped fighting and watched with wide eyes. Thus the Rama-Ravana battle continued uninterrupted for a night and a day. Then Shri Rama, with immense fury, strung a sharp arrow like a great venomous serpent and severed Ravana's head. All the worlds saw Ravana's head, adorned with glittering earrings, fall to the ground. Immediately, another head like the first sprouted from Ravana's body. Rama swiftly cut off that head too with another arrow. Again, as before, a head grew back. Then many heads cut by Shri Rama's arrows grew back with equal splendor, and Ravana was not slain. Rama, the joy of Kausalya, was a great hero skilled in all weapons and possessed of many missiles. Yet such Shri Rama fell into deep thought about how to slay that Rakshasa king. Question 5:
How did the destruction of Ravana take place?

Answer:
In that Rama-Ravana battle, Rama was only countering Ravana's arrows but not attempting to slay him. Matali thought, 'O Raghuvira! Why do you follow him like one who knows nothing? This one will die at the hands of a human. Therefore, slay him with the Brahmastra that has taken refuge in you.' Hearing the charioteer's words, Shri Rama remembered the Brahmastra. Immediately, that great hero took an arrow that hissed like a great serpent and blazed with light. Shri Rama, possessed of great strength and prowess, duly consecrated it with Vedic mantras, fixed the Brahmastra on his bow, drew it to his ear, and released that Brahmastra, which could sever the vital points, upon Ravana. That missile immediately struck Ravana's chest and pierced his heart. The next moment, having taken Ravana's life, that missile struck the earth and then, like a humble servant who has completed his duty, returned to Shri Rama's quiver. Then Ravana's bow, along with the arrows in his hand, fell to the ground along with his life. That great and powerful Rakshasa king, losing his life, fell from his chariot onto the battlefield like Vritrasura slain by the Vajrayudha. Then all beings rejoiced. Sugriva, Vibhishana, and Lakshmana, along with their followers, duly worshipped and praised Shri Rama on that battlefield.

Question 5:
After the war, when Hanuman visited Sita Devi, what kind of message did she send to Shri Rama through Maruti?

I will now go to that Lord. Please grant me your message.' Then Janaki Devi said, 'O foremost of Vanaras! I desire to see my lord.' Then Hanuman, with her permission, quickly went and stood before Shri Rama.

Question 4:
At the time when Sita Devi entered the fire, what words did Lord Brahma speak when he appeared?

Answer:
At the time when Sita Devi entered the fire, hearing the cries of lamentation of the Vanaras and Rakshasas, the righteous Sri Rama, greatly sorrowful, with tears flowing in agitation, fell into thought for a moment. Meanwhile, Brahma and the other Devas appeared and said, 'O Rama! You are the supreme among all. You are the foremost among the wise. How can you remain indifferent while Sita Devi is entering the fire? You are Sri Mahavishnu, the supreme among all Devas. You do not realize that fact.' Then Sri Rama, having saluted Brahma and the other Devas, said, 'As Sri Rama, the son of King Dasharatha, I consider myself an ordinary human being. But who am I? Whose am I? Why have I come? I pray that Lord Brahma may explain to me.' Then Lord Brahma said, 'O Rama of true valor! I will tell you the truth, listen. You are Narayana, the cause of the universe. You are endowed with the six divine attributes. This Sita Devi is indeed Lakshmi Devi. To slay Ravana, the tormentor of the worlds, you have incarnated as a human on earth. You have established Dharma and fulfilled the purpose of the Devas. By praising you, desired wishes are fulfilled.' Thus Lord Brahma himself told Sri Ramachandra.

Question 5:
In what manner did Agni Deva deliver Sita Devi, who had entered the fire, to Sri Rama?

Answer:
As soon as the auspicious words of Lord Brahma were heard, Agni Deva, holding Sita Devi like a father, rose from the fire pit, pushed aside the blazing kindling, came out and stood. Agni Deva delivered that Sita Devi, possessed of unparalleled beauty and splendor, to Sri Rama. Then that Agni Deva, the witness of the worlds, said, 'O Lord Ramachandra! There is no fault whatsoever in this Vaidehi, your lawful wife. She, with purity in thought, word, and deed, always meditates on you alone. There is no fault in her.' Hearing the words of Agni Deva, Rama thought, 'Alas! I have unjustly pained this most excellent woman with harsh words,' and, agitated, shed tears. After reflecting for a moment, he said to that foremost of Devas, 'O Havyavahana! There is no fault in Sita Devi. She is pure. But because she stayed for a long time in the shade of the Ashoka tree in Ravana's city, I had to conduct this test so that the people of the three worlds would not doubt her. If I had accepted her without any test to reveal the purity of this Ayoni-born one, the people would blame me, saying that Dasharatha's Rama is lustful and foolish. I, who am ever devoted to truth, remained indifferent while she entered the fire so that the people of the three worlds might trust Sita Devi's chastity.' Thus Sri Rama, drawing Sita Devi near, dwelt happily.

Question 5:
In what manner did Shri Rama's coronation take place?

Answer:
After everyone proceeded in a procession from Nandigrama to Ayodhya, they decorated the platform with the jeweled throne for Shri Rama's coronation in every way. The ministers, all government officials, city dignitaries who came to witness the coronation sat in seats appropriate to their status. Then, the family preceptor Shri Vasishtha, amidst the Vedic chanting of the Ritviks, seated Shri Sita-Ramachandra, adorned with all ornaments, on the jeweled throne. Thereafter, with auspicious musical instruments and Vedic mantras, Vasishtha, Vamadeva, Jabali, and other Maharshis anointed Shri Ramachandra, the Supreme Being, with pure fragrant waters, like the Vasus anointed Indra. Then, with Vedic mantras, they anointed Sita and Rama with sacred waters mixed with various herbal essences. Thereafter, the very first crown that Manu had placed on his head at his coronation, that crown worn by all lords of Ayodhya in hereditary succession, Shri Vasishtha Maharshi, with Vedic mantras, placed it on the head of Lord Ramachandra seated on the jeweled throne. Then the Ritviks also adorned the Lord with other ornaments. Thereafter, Shri Ramachandra, having become emperor, made Bharata the Yuvaraja, performed many Yajnas such as Ashvamedha, ruled the kingdom for many years, looking after the people like his own children.
